query.sentence: What non-PFAS additives or formulation strategies can be used in thermoplastics to achieve low coefficient of friction (COF), good release / anti stick behavior and low surface energy?

properties: Coefficient of friction (COF) < 0.25 on typical test surfaces
Surface energy ≤ 30 mN/m to promote non‑adhesive behavior
Thermal stability ≥ processing temperature of the host thermoplastic (≈ 160–200 °C for PEBA, PP, or PC)
Chemical compatibility with common thermoplastics (PEBA, PP, PC, PPS)
Non‑toxic and environmentally benign (no PFAS content)
Processability: additive must be melt‑blendable without causing phase separation or excessive viscosity rise
constraints: Additive must not contain fluorine atoms in C–F bonds (PFAS‑free)
Additive must not degrade under UV/thermal exposure typical of the application
Additive must not leach into food or medical environments if used in those sectors
Additive concentration should be ≤ 5 wt% to avoid compromising mechanical properties

final_candidate.material_name: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) – 3 wt% additive in PEBA or PP
final_candidate.justification: Patents Doc 1 and Doc 2 demonstrate that polydiorganosiloxanes (e.g., PDMS) incorporated at 2–4 wt% into PEBA or other thermoplastics reduce COF to <0.20 and improve release characteristics. The silicone backbone provides low surface energy (~25 mN/m) and excellent thermal stability (>300 °C). Literature from manufacturing textbooks confirms that silicone additives are melt‑blendable, do not form PFAS, and are widely used in release liners, coatings, and elastomer formulations.

process_recipe.description: Dry the base polymer (e.g., PEBA 2533) in a vacuum oven at 65 °C for 6 h to remove moisture.
Weigh PDMS (average Mn ≈ 2000 g/mol) to achieve a 3 wt% loading relative to the polymer.
Load dried PEBA and PDMS into a twin‑screw micro‑compounder (15 mL capacity). Set melt temperature to 160 °C, screw speed 200 rpm, cycle time 3 min.
Extrude the melt into rods (≈3.5 mm diameter) or films using a cast extrusion die.
Quench extrudate in a water bath, then air‑dry at room temperature for 24 h.
Test COF using a pinch test (Harland FTS 6000) or ASTM D1894 on the finished rod/film.
